text: The patient is positioned in the modified lithotomy position on the operating table. The surgeon is positioned on the right side of the patient, while the cameraman is on the left side of the surgeon, and the first assistant is on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum under the inferior mesenteric vein and performs a blunt dissection to separate the mesocolon from the prurinal fat tissue. The lymph node dissection is carried out at the root of the inferior mesenteric vein. A blunt dissection beneath the inferior mesenteric artery exposes the origin of the left colic artery, where a central tie is applied.

The surgeon isolates and divides the inferior mesenteric vein below the inferior border of the pancreas. The lateral attachments of the colon are incised up to the splenic flexure, followed by the division of the frenocolic ligament.

The surgeon performs an oncologic dissection of the splenic flexure using an anterior approach. The greater omentum is divided close to the stomach, and the dissection continues leftward until the splenocolic ligament is divided. The patient is in the anti-Trendelenburg position, with the cameraman positioned between the patient's legs using a thirty-degree camera angle. Full mobilization of the splenic flexure is achieved without additional trocars.

The surgeon performs dissection of the left transverse mesocolon from the inferior border of the pancreas, dividing the pancreatic ligament. Using diathermy, the mesocolon is separated from the spleen and retroperitoneum. The middle colic vessels are identified, and the left branch is divided. A supraumbilical laparotomy incision is made through the umbilical port to remove the specimen. The marginal artery is divided in its transverse and left colonic portion. A GIA device is used to divide the large intestine, followed by a hands-on lateral-lateral colicolic anastomosis, completing the specimen removal.
